description: Reading .agent/work/initial-gctx/decision.md

content.content:
1	# Initial `gctx` Public CLI
2	
3	## Objective
4	
5	Create a public Go command named `gctx` that makes a native Google Cloud CLI
6	configuration the single context users select while synchronizing the active
7	`gcloud` configuration, its human user account, Application Default
8	Credentials (ADC), and an explicit quota project. The command must hide the
9	multi-command authentication sequence behind a `kubectx`-style interface
10	without inventing a second configuration system.
11	
12	## Confirmed User Decisions
13	
14	- Create the public repository `github.com/kellen-miller/gctx` and its local
15	  checkout at `/Users/kellen/development/github/kellen-miller/gctx`.
16	- Implement the command in Go 1.26 with `github.com/urfave/cli/v3`.
17	- Discover native `gcloud` configurations dynamically. Do not hardcode any
18	  account, project, organization, or configuration name.
19	- Keep `gctx` a strict switcher. It must not create or edit `gcloud`
20	  configurations.
21	- Running `gctx` opens an `fzf` selector. Running
22	  `gctx <configuration>` switches directly. `gctx --current` prints the
23	  current configuration, and `gctx -` returns to the previous configuration.
24	- Require every selectable configuration to have explicit `core/account`,
25	  `core/project`, and `billing/quota_project` properties. Do not infer the
26	  quota project from `core/project`.
27	- Support human Google user accounts only in v1. Do not generate, copy, or
28	  activate service-account keys, and do not infer an impersonation or
29	  federation policy.
30	- Synchronize target ADC before activating the target CLI configuration. If
31	  synchronization fails, best-effort restore the prior ADC identity and quota,
32	  leave the active configuration and previous-context state unchanged, and
33	  report both the primary and rollback errors when rollback also fails.
34	- Persist the previous context so `gctx -` behaves like `kubectx -`.
35	- Officially support macOS and Linux in v1. Avoid deliberate Windows-only
36	  breakage but do not claim or test Windows support yet.
37	- Use the MIT license.
38	- Use GitHub-hosted runners for pull-request CI. CI must check formatting,
39	  linting/vulnerabilities, tests including the race detector, and a build.
40	- Do not publish a package to a registry or add an automated release pipeline.
41	  Document installation from the public Go module with `go install`.
42	- Push the implementation on a feature branch, open a pull request, observe
43	  its CI, and leave the green pull request open for user review.
44	
45	## Agent-Recommended Defaults
46	
47	- Put the `main` package at the module root so installation is
48	  `go install github.com/kellen-miller/gctx@latest` rather than requiring a
49	  `/cmd/gctx` suffix.
50	- Keep the public command thin. Put switching policy in one deep internal
51	  module whose interface exposes complete user operations rather than leaking
52	  individual `gcloud` steps to the CLI layer.
53	- Use the external `fzf` executable rather than embedding a second fuzzy finder
54	  or terminal UI. Direct selection remains usable when `fzf` is absent.
55	- Ask native `gcloud info` for the active Cloud SDK configuration directory,
56	  store a hidden `gctx` state file beside that directory's configuration data,
57	  and replace it atomically. This keeps previous-context state isolated when a
58	  user maintains multiple `CLOUDSDK_CONFIG` directories.
59	- Treat a non-empty `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` as an error because that
60	  override would make the ADC file updated by `gctx` ineffective.
61	- Treat a non-empty `CLOUDSDK_ACTIVE_CONFIG_NAME` as an error because the
62	  caller's environment would continue to override the configuration activated
63	  by the child `gcloud` process.
64	- Validate a human account conservatively as an email-like principal that is
65	  not a `gserviceaccount.com` address. Reject unsupported principal forms with
66	  an actionable error rather than guessing.
67	- Pin third-party GitHub Actions to full commit SHAs and use Ubuntu and macOS
68	  jobs. The local Weave repositories provide conventions for pinned actions,
69	  `go test -race`, `golangci-lint`, `govulncheck`, and explicit build checks,
70	  but Weave-only RunsOn runners and private reusable workflows are not suitable
71	  for this public personal repository.
72	- Add table-driven tests at the command's public behavior seam using fake
73	  `gcloud` and `fzf` executables on `PATH`. This verifies real argument order,
74	  JSON parsing, cancellation, rollback, and state without calling live cloud
75	  APIs from CI.
76	
77	## Assumptions
78	
79	- Users install and authenticate the Google Cloud CLI separately.
80	- Interactive selection requires `fzf` on `PATH`; direct selection does not.
81	- Native `gcloud` configuration activation is global to the selected Cloud SDK
82	  configuration directory, so simultaneous switches remain last-writer-wins.
83	- A user granting a project as an ADC quota project has
84	  `serviceusage.services.use` on that project. Native `gcloud` validates the
85	  permission and supplies the authoritative failure.
86	- `go install ...@latest` is sufficient distribution for the first public
87	  version; versioned releases and package-manager formulas are future work.
88	
89	## Open Questions or User Judgments
90	
91	None. The grill resolved the public interface, supported identities, platform
92	scope, failure behavior, license, distribution, CI, and rollout.
93	
94	## Accepted Risks and Failure Modes
95	
96	- ADC is one shared local credential file. A successful switch intentionally
97	  replaces its active identity and quota project.
98	- Native commands cannot provide a true transaction across CLI configuration
99	  and ADC. `gctx` minimizes partial state by validating first, activating last,
100	  and attempting rollback, but an interrupted process or failed rollback can
101	  still require the manual recovery commands printed by the tool.
102	- Previous-context state is a small file scoped to the native Cloud SDK
103	  configuration directory. Concurrent invocations may
104	  overwrite one another; this matches native `gcloud` activation semantics and
105	  does not justify a cross-process locking subsystem in v1.
106	- Service accounts, workforce identities, credential-file overrides, Windows,
107	  embedded fuzzy search, shell completion, binary releases, and package-manager
108	  installation are intentionally outside v1.
109	
110	## Validation Expectations
111	
112	- `go test -race ./...` passes with behavior tests for direct selection, fuzzy
113	  selection, cancellation, missing properties, unsupported principals,
114	  `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS`, successful synchronization and activation,
115	  quota failure rollback, rollback failure reporting, `--current`, and `-`.
116	- `golangci-lint run`, `govulncheck ./...`, `go vet ./...`, `gofmt` checking,
117	  and `go build ./...` pass locally and in pull-request CI.
118	- A manual smoke test against the user's real configurations proves that a
119	  switch aligns `gcloud config list`, ADC quota project, and `gctx --current`,
120	  and that `gctx -` returns to the prior configuration.
121	- README examples use placeholders and native `gcloud` commands only. They
122	  explain authentication, named configuration creation, explicit account,
123	  project and quota properties, `serviceusage.services.use`, ADC behavior,
124	  installation, prerequisites, commands, failure recovery, and limitations.
125	- The public GitHub pull request is open and every required check is green.
126	
127	## Source Notes
128	
129	This decision record compiles the current Codex conversation on 2026-07-12.
130	Repository evidence came from the local `weave-labs/ci`, `weave-labs/syngine`,
131	`weave-labs/weave-python`, `weave-labs/weave-go`, and `weave-labs/weave-cli`
132	checkouts. Current upstream evidence came from Google Cloud CLI/ADC
133	documentation, the Go 1.26 release documentation, GitHub Actions setup-go
134	documentation, the official `fzf` repository, and the `urfave/cli` v3 guide.
135	
136	The worktree is
137	`/Users/kellen/development/github/kellen-miller/gctx/.worktrees/initial-gctx`
138	on branch `feat/initial-gctx`, based on `main` commit `566642f`. The branch has
139	no upstream until it is pushed with an explicit remote head ref.
140	
141	`CONTEXT.md` and ADRs are intentionally skipped: this is a small greenfield
142	command with one resolved public interface, and the decision record plus
143	ExecPlan are sufficient durable context.
144

description: Reading .agent/work/initial-gctx/execplan.md

content.content:
1	# Build and publish the initial `gctx` command
2	
3	This ExecPlan is a living document. The sections `Progress`, `Surprises &
4	Discoveries`, `Decision Log`, and `Outcomes & Retrospective` must be kept up to
5	date as work proceeds. Maintain this document in accordance with
6	`.agent/PLANS.md` from the repository root.
7	
8	## Purpose / Big Picture
9	
10	After this work, a developer with several native Google Cloud CLI
11	configurations can run `gctx` to fuzzy-select one, run `gctx NAME` to select it
12	directly, or run `gctx -` to return to the previous one. A successful switch
13	aligns the active native `gcloud` configuration with the same human account in
14	Application Default Credentials (ADC) and with that configuration's explicit
15	quota project. The user no longer has to remember and sequence separate
16	configuration, login, ADC, and quota commands.
17	
18	The visible proof is a public `github.com/kellen-miller/gctx` repository, an
19	installable Go command, behavior tests that simulate native executables, a
20	manual smoke test against real local configurations, and an open pull request
21	whose formatting, lint, vulnerability, test, and build checks are green.
22	
23	The important complexity dividend is that authentication order, validation,
24	rollback, fuzzy selection, and previous-context state live behind one deep
25	module. The `urfave/cli` command layer asks for complete operations such as
26	switching or reporting the current context; it never orchestrates individual
27	`gcloud` subprocesses.
28	
29	## Progress
30	
31	- [x] (2026-07-12 14:54Z) Completed the grill and recorded its decisions in
32	  `decision.md`.
33	- [x] (2026-07-12 14:54Z) Created the public repository, established `main` at
34	  `566642f`, and created the isolated `feat/initial-gctx` worktree.
35	- [x] (2026-07-12 14:58Z) Copied `.agent/PLANS.md` and drafted this ExecPlan.
36	- [ ] Review and improve the planning packet, then activate implementation.
37	- [ ] Implement the command behavior test-first.
38	- [ ] Add contributor tasks, public documentation, license metadata, and PR CI.
39	- [ ] Validate locally against fake executables and real Google Cloud contexts.
40	- [ ] Review the completed implementation and resolve adversarial findings.
41	- [ ] Commit, push with an explicit remote head ref, open the public pull
42	  request, and leave it open after all checks pass.
43	
44	## Surprises & Discoveries
45	
46	- Observation: the live default configuration had a Weave account paired with
47	  a personal project and quota project after a native account login, which
48	  reproduces the cross-context drift `gctx` must prevent.
49	  Evidence: `gcloud config configurations describe default --format=json`
50	  reported `core.account=kellen@weavelabs.io` and
51	  `core.project=billing.quota_project=chief-discord-bot-502202`.
52	- Observation: native configuration listing can project only the fields `gctx`
53	  needs, avoiding both the enormous default property tree and one subprocess
54	  per configuration.
55	  Evidence: the live command `gcloud config configurations list
56	  --format='json(name,is_active,properties.core.account,properties.core.project,properties.billing.quota_project)'`
57	  returned one sparse object per configuration with exactly the requested
58	  nested properties.
59	- Observation: `gcloud auth application-default set-quota-project` removes the
60	  quota value while checking permission but restores the previous value when
61	  permission is denied. It does not restore the ADC identity written by the
62	  preceding login step.
63	  Evidence: the installed SDK's `auth_util.AddQuotaProjectToADC` saves
64	  `previous_quota_project`, checks `serviceusage.services.use`, and rewrites
65	  that previous value before raising; identity rollback remains `gctx`'s job.
66	
67	## Decision Log
68	
69	- Decision: use Go 1.26 and `github.com/urfave/cli/v3`.
70	  Rationale: the user explicitly chose a standalone Go command and this CLI
71	  framework; the root `main` package keeps `go install` concise.
72	  Date/Author: 2026-07-12 / user and Codex.
73	- Decision: treat native named configurations as the only configuration source
74	  of truth.
75	  Rationale: a second profile store would duplicate account/project policy and
76	  create drift. `gctx` validates and switches but never creates or edits.
77	  Date/Author: 2026-07-12 / user.
78	- Decision: require explicit `billing/quota_project` and human user accounts.
79	  Rationale: inferred quota projects and guessed service-account policies would
80	  violate the tool's correctness promise.
81	  Date/Author: 2026-07-12 / user.
82	- Decision: synchronize target ADC before native activation and best-effort
83	  restore the previous ADC on failure.
84	  Rationale: native commands do not provide a transaction, so sequencing and
85	  rollback must minimize partial state and make residual damage observable.
86	  Date/Author: 2026-07-12 / user.
87	- Decision: store previous-context state in the Cloud SDK configuration
88	  directory returned by `gcloud info`.
89	  Rationale: state then follows `CLOUDSDK_CONFIG` instead of colliding across
90	  multiple independent SDK directories.
91	  Date/Author: 2026-07-12 / Codex, based on live native behavior.
92	- Decision: reject `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` and
93	  `CLOUDSDK_ACTIVE_CONFIG_NAME` overrides.
94	  Rationale: either override would cause the caller's effective context to
95	  disagree with the state `gctx` updates.
96	  Date/Author: 2026-07-12 / Codex.
97	- Decision: support macOS and Linux, external `fzf`, MIT licensing, GitHub-only
98	  Go installation, and GitHub-hosted PR CI; leave the green PR open.
99	  Rationale: these are the explicit v1 product and rollout boundaries.
100	  Date/Author: 2026-07-12 / user.
101	
102	## Outcomes & Retrospective
103	
104	Implementation has not started. At completion, update this section with the
105	observable command behavior, test counts, manual smoke-test contexts, pull
106	request URL and checks, remaining limitations, and any lessons from native
107	`gcloud` behavior.
108	
109	## Context and Orientation
110	
111	The repository currently contains GitHub's initial `README.md`, an MIT
112	`LICENSE`, `.gitignore`, and this `.agent` planning tree. There is no Go module
113	or source code yet. The worktree is
114	`/Users/kellen/development/github/kellen-miller/gctx/.worktrees/initial-gctx`
115	on `feat/initial-gctx`, based on `main` commit `566642f`, with no upstream until
116	the branch is pushed.
117	
118	A Google Cloud CLI configuration is a native named set of properties. The
119	required properties are `core/account`, `core/project`, and
120	`billing/quota_project`. ADC is the separate local credential file used by
121	Google client libraries; the `gcloud` CLI itself does not use ADC. A quota
122	project attributes quota and billing for client-based APIs and requires the ADC
123	principal to hold `serviceusage.services.use` on that project.
124	
125	The root `main.go` will be the installed command. `internal/app` will bind
126	`urfave/cli` arguments to the use-case interface. `internal/gctx` will be the
127	deep module that owns native configuration discovery, validation, fuzzy
128	selection, account/ADC/quota synchronization, activation, rollback, and
129	previous-state persistence. Split its implementation into focused files only
130	to improve locality; do not expose each file as another caller-facing concept.
131	
132	The production implementation invokes `gcloud` and `fzf` through `os/exec`
133	with argument arrays, never through a shell. Tests place deterministic fake
134	executables in a temporary `PATH`, allowing the same public command path to be
135	exercised without real credentials or cloud access. This is the agreed test
136	seam: tests observe commands, output, state, and exit behavior rather than
137	reaching into sequencing internals.
138	
139	## Plan of Work
140	
141	### Milestone 1: Establish a testable Go command skeleton
142	
143	Create `go.mod` with module path `github.com/kellen-miller/gctx`, Go 1.26, and
144	the current v3 release of `github.com/urfave/cli/v3`. Put the executable package
145	at `main.go` so `go install github.com/kellen-miller/gctx@latest` installs the
146	`gctx` binary directly. Derive the displayed version from
147	`runtime/debug.ReadBuildInfo`, using `devel` for local unversioned builds.
148	
149	Create `internal/app/app.go` with a small use-case interface containing the
150	complete operations needed by the CLI: select-and-switch, switch-by-name,
151	switch-to-previous, and current. Construct a `cli.Command` that supports no
152	positional argument, exactly one configuration name, the special `-` argument,
153	`--current` (with `-c`), `--help`, and `--version`. Reject extra positional
154	arguments.
155	Cancellation from `fzf` exits successfully without changing state; genuine
156	interrupts use exit code 130; validation or native-command failures use exit
157	code 1 with a concise error on stderr.
158	
159	The app writes only the stable result summary. The manager suppresses routine
160	native success output, but it must attach the terminal for `gcloud auth login`
161	so an expired credential can display its browser URL or prompt. Error output
162	may include native diagnostics after `gctx`'s contextual message. Never invoke
163	an access-token command or capture an access token.
164	
165	Write `internal/app/app_test.go` before the implementation using a fake
166	use-case adapter. These tests own parsing, dispatch, output, help/version, extra
167	arguments, cancellation, and exit-code behavior. At the end of this milestone,
168	`go test ./internal/app` passes while the actual switcher is still absent.
169	
170	### Milestone 2: Implement native configuration discovery and validation
171	
172	Create the `internal/gctx` module. Its small caller-facing interface should be
173	represented by a `Manager` exposing complete operations, not individual
174	subprocess steps. Keep a private command-runner seam that can stream stdin,
175	stdout, and stderr for interactive auth commands or capture stdout for JSON and
176	value queries. Provide the production `os/exec` adapter and a deterministic
177	fake adapter in tests.
178	
179	Use one native command to locate and inspect configuration state:
180	
181	    gcloud config configurations list --format=json(name,is_active,properties.core.account,properties.core.project,properties.billing.quota_project)
182	    gcloud info --format=value(config.paths.global_config_dir)
183	
184	Decode only the projected fields required by the tool and identify the current
185	configuration from `is_active`. Validation must
186	reject missing account, project, or explicit quota project and print exact
187	native repair commands scoped with `--configuration=NAME`. Reject accounts
188	that are not email-like human principals or that end in
189	`.gserviceaccount.com`. Reject non-empty `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` and
190	`CLOUDSDK_ACTIVE_CONFIG_NAME` before any mutation. Detect missing `gcloud`
191	early and report its prerequisite.
192	
193	Create table-driven tests for configuration not found, every missing property,
194	unsupported principals, environment overrides, sparse JSON parsing, malformed
195	native output, and command failures. The tests must assert actionable errors
196	without embedding any real account or project from the user's machine.
197	
198	### Milestone 3: Add fuzzy selection and previous-context state
199	
200	For no-argument use, turn the projected native configuration list into `fzf`
201	rows containing name, account, project, and quota columns. Mark incomplete
202	entries visibly rather than hiding them; selecting one sends it through the
203	same strict validation path as direct selection. Invoke external `fzf` with
204	stdin/stdout attached appropriately and parse the selected configuration from
205	the first tab-separated field. Treat fzf exit 1 (no match) and 130 (cancel or
206	interrupt) as no-op cancellation when no selection was emitted; other nonzero
207	codes are errors. A missing `fzf` blocks only interactive selection and
208	suggests its native install documentation; direct selection continues to work.
209	
210	Create a private state type containing the previous configuration name. Resolve
211	its directory through `gcloud info` and use the fixed filename
212	`.gctx-state.json`. Stage updates in the same directory with mode `0600`,
213	`fsync`, and atomically rename only after a successful native activation.
214	Temporary files use a `.gctx-state-*.tmp` pattern and are removed on every
215	error path. If staging fails, abort before credential changes. Switching from
216	A to B writes A as previous; `gctx -` switches B to A and writes B as previous,
217	so repeated `gctx -` toggles. Selecting the already-current configuration
218	resynchronizes ADC but does not overwrite previous state.
219	
220	Tests must cover display rows, selection, cancellation, malformed selections,
221	missing `fzf`, first switch without state, toggle behavior, same-context
222	resynchronization, file permissions, atomic replacement, corrupt state, and
223	state isolation across two fake Cloud SDK directories.
224	
225	### Milestone 4: Hide synchronization, activation, and rollback sequencing
226	
227	Implement one manager operation that accepts a validated target and hides the
228	entire sequence. Capture the current configuration and validate it when
229	possible for rollback, but do not prevent a valid target from repairing a
230	currently incomplete context. Stage any next state before credentials change. Synchronize the
231	target without changing the current CLI property:
232	
233	    gcloud auth login ACCOUNT --brief --no-activate --update-adc --configuration=NAME
234	    gcloud auth application-default set-quota-project QUOTA --configuration=NAME
235	
236	Only after both commands succeed, activate the target:
237	
238	    gcloud config configurations activate NAME --quiet
239	
240	Then commit the staged previous-state file. If target login or quota setup
241	fails, use the validated prior configuration's account and quota to restore ADC
242	with the same two commands and leave CLI activation/state untouched. If native
243	activation or state commit fails after target ADC succeeds, reactivate the
244	prior configuration when necessary and restore its ADC. Join primary and
245	rollback errors so neither is hidden, and include exact manual commands when
246	automatic restoration cannot complete. Never print tokens, ADC JSON, or refresh
247	credentials.
248	
249	Drive this milestone test-first with command transcripts. Cover successful
250	order, expired-login passthrough, quota denial, target activation failure,
251	state-commit failure, successful rollback, failed rollback, missing or invalid
252	prior configuration, same-context synchronization, and interruption. Assert
253	that failure never reports success and that previous state changes only on a
254	fully successful switch.
255	
256	### Milestone 5: Add contributor workflow, public documentation, and CI
257	
258	Update `.gitignore` for local binaries, coverage, and `dist` while preserving
259	the already-ignored `.worktrees`. Add a local `.golangci.yaml`. Use Go 1.26
260	tool dependencies in `go.mod` for locked golangci-lint, govulncheck, and
261	actionlint versions rather than downloading unversioned tools during CI. Add
262	`Taskfile.yaml` targets `fmt`, `lint`, `test`, `build`, and `check`, while
263	keeping the raw Go commands documented for contributors who do not install
264	Task. `lint` includes the GitHub Actions workflow check.
265	
266	Replace the generated `README.md` with public documentation. It must explain
267	the problem, prerequisites, installation via `go install`, every command,
268	external `fzf`, human-account and macOS/Linux scope, shared ADC semantics,
269	environment override rejections, state location, rollback limits, and removal.
270	Its setup tutorial must use placeholders and only native commands:
271	
272	    gcloud auth login ACCOUNT
273	    gcloud config configurations create NAME
274	    gcloud config set account ACCOUNT --configuration=NAME
275	    gcloud config set project PROJECT --configuration=NAME
276	    gcloud config set billing/quota_project QUOTA_PROJECT --configuration=NAME
277	
278	Explain that the account needs `serviceusage.services.use`, commonly through
279	`roles/serviceusage.serviceUsageConsumer`, on the quota project. Explain how to
280	list accounts, projects, organizations and configurations; inspect a
281	configuration; recover CLI and ADC manually; and verify the selected CLI
282	properties and ADC quota without exposing credentials. State that users do not
283	need to run `gcloud auth application-default login` during setup because a
284	successful `gctx` switch writes ADC from the cached CLI credential and then
285	sets its quota project.
286	
287	Add `.github/workflows/ci.yaml` triggered by pull requests with read-only
288	contents permission and GitHub-hosted runners. Pin checkout and setup-go actions
289	to full commit SHAs. Use separate named jobs for format/lint/vulnerability,
290	tests, and build. Test and build on both `ubuntu-latest` and `macos-latest` with
291	the Go version read from `go.mod`; run the race detector. CI must use the locked
292	Go tool dependencies, not Weave-only reusable workflows, private credentials,
293	or RunsOn runners.
294	
295	### Milestone 6: Validate, review, publish the branch, and observe CI
296	
297	Run the complete local checks from the worktree. Build a temporary binary and
298	exercise help/version/current plus fake end-to-end scenarios. Then use native
299	`gcloud` commands to create or correct the user's four local configurations
300	outside the repository: `weavelabs-dev`, `weavelabs-prod`,
301	`weavelabs-common`, and `personal-chief`, each with its known account, project,
302	and matching explicit quota project. This local setup is smoke-test data only
303	and must never appear in source, fixtures, snapshots, or README examples.
304	
305	Install or run the local binary and smoke-test a switch between one Weave
306	context and `personal-chief`, inspect CLI account/project/quota and the ADC
307	quota field, then run `gctx -` and verify the prior context is restored. Do not
308	print access tokens or refresh credentials. Restore the user's preferred
309	context after testing and record only redacted evidence in this plan.
310	
311	Run the required fresh review and adversarial implementation review, resolve
312	verified findings, and rerun checks. Commit only intended paths using
313	Conventional Commits. Push with:
314	
315	    git push origin HEAD:refs/heads/feat/initial-gctx
316	
317	Open a pull request to `main` describing behavior and validation. Watch every
318	check to completion, diagnose failures from live job logs, fix and push as
319	needed, and leave the green pull request open for user review. Do not merge it,
320	create a release, or publish binaries/packages.
321	
322	## Concrete Steps
323	
324	Run all implementation commands from
325	`/Users/kellen/development/github/kellen-miller/gctx/.worktrees/initial-gctx`.
326	Use test-driven slices rather than generating the whole repository at once.
327	
328	Initialize and maintain the module with:
329	
330	    go mod init github.com/kellen-miller/gctx
331	    go get github.com/urfave/cli/v3@v3.10.1
332	    go get -tool github.com/golangci/golangci-lint/v2/cmd/golangci-lint@latest
333	    go get -tool golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck@latest
334	    go get -tool github.com/rhysd/actionlint/cmd/actionlint@latest
335	    go mod tidy
336	
337	Once project tasks exist, use:
338	
339	    task fmt
340	    task lint
341	    task test
342	    task build
343	    task check
344	
345	The equivalent final raw checks are:
346	
347	    test -z "$(gofmt -l .)"
348	    go vet ./...
349	    go tool golangci-lint run
350	    go tool govulncheck ./...
351	    go tool actionlint
352	    go test -race ./...
353	    go build -trimpath ./...
354	
355	Run the command during development with:
356	
357	    go run . --help
358	    go run . --version
359	    go run . --current
360	
361	Do not run live switching until fake-executable tests, the full local check,
362	and implementation review pass. Before live testing, inspect configurations
363	with:
364	
365	    gcloud config configurations list
366	    gcloud config configurations describe NAME
367	
368	Expected successful output is concise and contains the selected configuration,
369	account, project, and quota project but no tokens. Expected validation failures
370	name the configuration and provide the precise `gcloud config set` repair
371	command. Expected `fzf` cancellation produces no context change and no error.
372	A successful switch should render a stable shape such as:
373	
374	    Switched to example-dev
375	    Account: user@example.com
376	    Project: example-dev
377	    Quota project: example-dev-quota
378	
379	`gctx --current` and `gctx -c` print only the current configuration name so
380	they remain script-friendly.
381	
382	## Validation and Acceptance
383	
384	The implementation is accepted only when all of the following are observable.
385	The repository is public and cloneable. `go install
386	github.com/kellen-miller/gctx@latest` is a documented installation path after
387	the implementation reaches `main`; until the PR is merged, build or install the
388	checked-out source locally with `go install .`.
389	
390	`go test -race ./...` passes every behavior named in `decision.md` without live
391	cloud access. Formatting, vet, golangci-lint, govulncheck, actionlint, and build
392	all exit zero. `go run . --help`, `--version`, and `--current` show stable
393	public output.
394	Direct and fuzzy switches produce the same native command transcript in tests.
395	Failures prove rollback and never update previous state prematurely.
396	
397	The README contains no personal identifiers or Weave-specific project values.
398	Search with:
399	
400	    rg -n 'kellen|weavelabs|chief-discord' --glob '!\.agent/**' .
401	
402	and expect no matches in public product files. The MIT license remains present.
403	The only persistent tool state is the previous native configuration name beside
404	the selected Cloud SDK directory, with restrictive permissions.
405	
406	A real smoke switch aligns these observations:
407	
408	    gcloud config configurations list --filter=is_active:true --format=value(name)
409	    gcloud config list --format=json
410	    jq -r .quota_project_id "$(gcloud info --format=value(config.paths.global_config_dir))/application_default_credentials.json"
411	
412	The first command equals `gctx --current`, the listed account/project/quota
413	match the selected configuration, and ADC quota equals its explicit quota
414	project. Running `gctx -` restores the prior configuration and ADC pairing.
415	
416	The public pull request remains open and every GitHub Actions job is green.
417	Live `gh pr checks` output, not merely local workflow inspection, is the final
418	CI evidence.
419	
420	## Idempotence and Recovery
421	
422	All read and validation operations are repeatable. Selecting the already-active
423	configuration resynchronizes ADC but does not destroy the stored previous
424	context. Atomic state replacement prevents a partially written JSON file from
425	becoming authoritative.
426	
427	If target authentication fails before ADC is written, retry after completing
428	the browser flow. If quota setup fails, grant the selected human account
429	`serviceusage.services.use` on the explicit quota project and retry. The
430	manager attempts to restore the previous ADC on any later failure. When that
431	rollback fails, the error must print the exact prior account, configuration,
432	and quota commands needed to recover, but never a credential value.
433	
434	If the active configuration becomes wrong, recover with:
435	
436	    gcloud config configurations activate PREVIOUS_NAME
437	
438	If ADC identity or quota becomes wrong, recover with:
439	
440	    gcloud auth login PREVIOUS_ACCOUNT --brief --no-activate --update-adc --configuration=PREVIOUS_NAME
441	    gcloud auth application-default set-quota-project PREVIOUS_QUOTA --configuration=PREVIOUS_NAME
442	
443	The feature worktree can be removed after the PR is merged or abandoned without
444	affecting the primary checkout. Do not delete local Google Cloud configurations
445	during testing; correct them with native `gcloud config set` commands.
446	
447	## Artifacts and Notes
448	
449	Planning evidence and reviews live under `.agent/work/initial-gctx`. Store the
450	planning adversarial review at
451	`.agent/work/initial-gctx/adversarial/plan-review.md` and the implementation
452	review at `.agent/work/initial-gctx/adversarial/implementation-review.md`, with
453	their stream and auth artifacts beside them.
454	
455	The local exemplar choices are deliberate. `weave-labs/ci` demonstrates pinned
456	actions, separate Go lint/test jobs, `govulncheck`, the race detector, and
457	setup-go. `weave-labs/weave-cli` demonstrates `urfave/cli/v3`, a root command
458	that maps errors to exit codes, Task-based local checks, and build metadata.
459	The new public repository must not inherit RunsOn, private GitHub App auth,
460	private module setup, or Weave-specific central lint downloads.
461	
462	## Interfaces and Dependencies
463	
464	The module path is `github.com/kellen-miller/gctx` with Go 1.26. The only
465	runtime Go dependency is `github.com/urfave/cli/v3`. External executable
466	dependencies are `gcloud` for all operations and `fzf` only for no-argument
467	interactive selection.
468	
469	In `internal/app/app.go`, define an unexported use-case interface shaped around
470	complete behavior:
471	
472	    type contexts interface {
473	        SelectAndSwitch(context.Context) (gctx.Result, error)
474	        Switch(context.Context, string) (gctx.Result, error)
475	        SwitchPrevious(context.Context) (gctx.Result, error)
476	        Current(context.Context) (string, error)
477	    }
478	
479	Expose `gctx.ErrSelectionCanceled` as the single domain sentinel the command
480	layer handles as no-op success. The command layer must not learn subprocess
481	exit codes, state paths, or authentication order.
482	
483	In `internal/gctx/manager.go`, expose a `Manager` with the four corresponding
484	operations. `Result` contains only safe display fields: configuration name,
485	account, project, and quota project. The module hides configuration parsing,
486	validation, selection, state staging, auth/ADC/quota order, activation, and
487	rollback. This is the primary deep module and the test interface.
488	
489	Keep the subprocess runner interface private to `internal/gctx`; it needs
490	capture and interactive execution modes with context cancellation. Production
491	uses `os/exec`, while tests use both a fake adapter and fake executables on
492	`PATH`. Keep state-file implementation private and test it through manager
493	behavior plus focused filesystem tests. Do not create exported packages for
494	single-call wrappers. The implementation performs no direct Google HTTP calls,
495	collects no telemetry, and reads no credential JSON; native `gcloud` remains
496	the sole owner of credentials and permission checks.
497	
498	Revision note (2026-07-12): Initial plan compiled from the completed grill,
499	local repository evidence, installed Google SDK behavior, and current upstream
500	documentation. It resolves module shape, command behavior, transaction order,
501	test seams, documentation, CI, live validation, and PR rollout before coding.
502	
503	Revision note (2026-07-12, improvement pass 1): Replaced per-configuration
504	describe calls with one verified projected JSON listing, made cancellation
505	codes explicit, allowed repair when the current configuration is incomplete,
506	and corrected the pre-merge install and live inspection commands.
507	
508	Revision note (2026-07-12, improvement pass 2): Added script-friendly current
509	output, locked actionlint coverage, neutral output examples, and clarified that
510	`gctx` creates ADC during switching rather than requiring a separate initial
511	ADC login.
512	
513	Revision note (2026-07-12, improvement pass 3): Made the app/manager seam and
514	cancellation contract explicit, fixed the secure state filename and temporary
515	file lifecycle, and constrained credential handling and native output without
516	adding shallow packages.
517

description: Reading .agent/PLANS.md

content.content:
1	# Codex Execution Plans (ExecPlans):
2	
3	This document describes the requirements for an execution plan ("ExecPlan"), a design document that a coding agent can follow to deliver a working feature or system change. Treat the reader as a complete beginner to this repository: they have only the current working tree and the single ExecPlan file you provide. There is no memory of prior plans and no external context.
4	
5	## How to use ExecPlans and PLANS.md
6	
7	When authoring an executable specification (ExecPlan), follow PLANS.md _to the letter_. If it is not in your context, refresh your memory by reading the entire PLANS.md file. Be thorough in reading (and re-reading) source material to produce an accurate specification. When creating a spec, start from the skeleton and flesh it out as you do your research.
8	
9	When implementing an executable specification (ExecPlan), do not prompt the user for "next steps"; simply proceed to the next milestone. Keep all sections up to date, add or split entries in the list at every stopping point to affirmatively state the progress made and next steps. Resolve ambiguities autonomously, and commit frequently.
10	
11	When discussing an executable specification (ExecPlan), record decisions in a log in the spec for posterity; it should be unambiguously clear why any change to the specification was made. ExecPlans are living documents, and it should always be possible to restart from _only_ the ExecPlan and no other work.
12	
13	When researching a design with challenging requirements or significant unknowns, use milestones to implement proof of concepts, "toy implementations", etc., that allow validating whether the user's proposal is feasible. Read the source code of libraries by finding or acquiring them, research deeply, and include prototypes to guide a fuller implementation.
14	
15	## Requirements
16	
17	NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENTS:
18	
19	* Every ExecPlan must be fully self-contained. Self-contained means that in its current form it contains all knowledge and instructions needed for a novice to succeed.
20	* Every ExecPlan is a living document. Contributors are required to revise it as progress is made, as discoveries occur, and as design decisions are finalized. Each revision must remain fully self-contained.
21	* Every ExecPlan must enable a complete novice to implement the feature end-to-end without prior knowledge of this repo.
22	* Every ExecPlan must produce a demonstrably working behavior, not merely code changes to "meet a definition".
23	* Every ExecPlan must define every term of art in plain language or do not use it.
24	
25	Purpose and intent come first. Begin by explaining, in a few sentences, why the work matters from a user's perspective: what someone can do after this change that they could not do before, and how to see it working. Then guide the reader through the exact steps to achieve that outcome, including what to edit, what to run, and what they should observe.
26	
27	The agent executing your plan can list files, read files, search, run the project, and run tests. It does not know any prior context and cannot infer what you meant from earlier milestones. Repeat any assumption you rely on. Do not point to external blogs or docs; if knowledge is required, embed it in the plan itself in your own words. If an ExecPlan builds upon a prior ExecPlan and that file is checked in, incorporate it by reference. If it is not, you must include all relevant context from that plan.
28	
29	## Formatting
30	
31	Format and envelope are simple and strict. Each ExecPlan must be one single fenced code block labeled as `md` that begins and ends with triple backticks. Do not nest additional triple-backtick code fences inside; when you need to show commands, transcripts, diffs, or code, present them as indented blocks within that single fence. Use indentation for clarity rather than code fences inside an ExecPlan to avoid prematurely closing the ExecPlan's code fence. Use two newlines after every heading, use # and ## and so on, and correct syntax for ordered and unordered lists.
32	
33	When writing an ExecPlan to a Markdown (.md) file where the content of the file *is only* the single ExecPlan, you should omit the triple backticks.
34	
35	Write in plain prose. Prefer sentences over lists. Avoid checklists, tables, and long enumerations unless brevity would obscure meaning. Checklists are permitted only in the `Progress` section, where they are mandatory. Narrative sections must remain prose-first.
36	
37	## Guidelines
38	
39	Self-containment and plain language are paramount. If you introduce a phrase that is not ordinary English ("daemon", "middleware", "RPC gateway", "filter graph"), define it immediately and remind the reader how it manifests in this repository (for example, by naming the files or commands where it appears). Do not say "as defined previously" or "according to the architecture doc." Include the needed explanation here, even if you repeat yourself.
40	
41	Avoid common failure modes. Do not rely on undefined jargon. Do not describe "the letter of a feature" so narrowly that the resulting code compiles but does nothing meaningful. Do not outsource key decisions to the reader. When ambiguity exists, resolve it in the plan itself and explain why you chose that path. Err on the side of over-explaining user-visible effects and under-specifying incidental implementation details.
42	
43	Anchor the plan with observable outcomes. State what the user can do after implementation, the commands to run, and the outputs they should see. Acceptance should be phrased as behavior a human can verify ("after starting the server, navigating to [http://localhost:8080/health](http://localhost:8080/health) returns HTTP 200 with body OK") rather than internal attributes ("added a HealthCheck struct"). If a change is internal, explain how its impact can still be demonstrated (for example, by running tests that fail before and pass after, and by showing a scenario that uses the new behavior).
44	
45	Specify repository context explicitly. Name files with full repository-relative paths, name functions and modules precisely, and describe where new files should be created. If touching multiple areas, include a short orientation paragraph that explains how those parts fit together so a novice can navigate confidently. When running commands, show the working directory and exact command line. When outcomes depend on environment, state the assumptions and provide alternatives when reasonable.
46	
47	Be idempotent and safe. Write the steps so they can be run multiple times without causing damage or drift. If a step can fail halfway, include how to retry or adapt. If a migration or destructive operation is necessary, spell out backups or safe fallbacks. Prefer additive, testable changes that can be validated as you go.
48	
49	Validation is not optional. Include instructions to run tests, to start the system if applicable, and to observe it doing something useful. Describe comprehensive testing for any new features or capabilities. Include expected outputs and error messages so a novice can tell success from failure. Where possible, show how to prove that the change is effective beyond compilation (for example, through a small end-to-end scenario, a CLI invocation, or an HTTP request/response transcript). State the exact test commands appropriate to the project’s toolchain and how to interpret their results.
50	
51	Capture evidence. When your steps produce terminal output, short diffs, or logs, include them inside the single fenced block as indented examples. Keep them concise and focused on what proves success. If you need to include a patch, prefer file-scoped diffs or small excerpts that a reader can recreate by following your instructions rather than pasting large blobs.
52	
53	## Design quality lens
54	
55	Use John Ousterhout's design philosophy as the default lens for shaping the plan and resolving design ambiguity.
56	
57	Prefer simple mental models over elegant-looking structure. Prefer deep modules over shallow wrappers. Prefer interfaces that hide sequencing and policy details. Prefer fewer concepts, fewer knobs, and fewer special cases. Prefer moving complexity behind a stable boundary over redistributing it across more files.
58	
59	Treat these as the main forms of complexity:
60	
61	* Change amplification: one logical change requires edits in many places.
62	* Cognitive load: a reader or caller must hold too many facts in mind.
63	* Unknown unknowns: important behavior is surprising, implicit, or scattered.
64	
65	Every ExecPlan should explain the design quality of the proposed change, not just the mechanics. Make it clear:
66	
67	* what complexity exists today and who pays for it
68	* what boundary, module, or interface becomes simpler after the change
69	* what knowledge, sequencing, or policy moves out of callers and into the implementation
70	* what concepts, branches, or special cases disappear
71	* what the complexity dividend is for future readers and future changes
72	
73	Do not mistake motion for simplification. A plan is weaker if it mainly adds wrappers, adapters, flags, layers, or configuration without hiding more detail from the rest of the system. If a new abstraction is required, state exactly what it hides and why the system is simpler with it than without it.
74	
75	## Milestones
76	
77	Milestones are narrative, not bureaucracy. If you break the work into milestones, introduce each with a brief paragraph that describes the scope, what will exist at the end of the milestone that did not exist before, the commands to run, and the acceptance you expect to observe. Keep it readable as a story: goal, work, result, proof. Progress and milestones are distinct: milestones tell the story, progress tracks granular work. Both must exist. Never abbreviate a milestone merely for the sake of brevity, do not leave out details that could be crucial to a future implementation.
78	
79	Each milestone must be independently verifiable and incrementally implement the overall goal of the execution plan.
80	
81	## Living plans and design decisions
82	
83	* ExecPlans are living documents. As you make key design decisions, update the plan to record both the decision and the thinking behind it. Record all decisions in the `Decision Log` section.
84	* ExecPlans must contain and maintain a `Progress` section, a `Surprises & Discoveries` section, a `Decision Log`, and an `Outcomes & Retrospective` section. These are not optional.
85	* When you discover optimizer behavior, performance tradeoffs, unexpected bugs, or inverse/unapply semantics that shaped your approach, capture those observations in the `Surprises & Discoveries` section with short evidence snippets (test output is ideal).
86	* If you change course mid-implementation, document why in the `Decision Log` and reflect the implications in `Progress`. Plans are guides for the next contributor as much as checklists for you.
87	* At completion of a major task or the full plan, write an `Outcomes & Retrospective` entry summarizing what was achieved, what remains, and lessons learned.
88	
89	# Prototyping milestones and parallel implementations
90	
91	It is acceptable—-and often encouraged—-to include explicit prototyping milestones when they de-risk a larger change. Examples: adding a low-level operator to a dependency to validate feasibility, or exploring two composition orders while measuring optimizer effects. Keep prototypes additive and testable. Clearly label the scope as “prototyping”; describe how to run and observe results; and state the criteria for promoting or discarding the prototype.
92	
93	Prefer additive code changes followed by subtractions that keep tests passing. Parallel implementations (e.g., keeping an adapter alongside an older path during migration) are fine when they reduce risk or enable tests to continue passing during a large migration. Describe how to validate both paths and how to retire one safely with tests. When working with multiple new libraries or feature areas, consider creating spikes that evaluate the feasibility of these features _independently_ of one another, proving that the external library performs as expected and implements the features we need in isolation.
94	
95	## Skeleton of a Good ExecPlan
96	
97	    # <Short, action-oriented description>
98	
99	    This ExecPlan is a living document. The sections `Progress`, `Surprises & Discoveries`, `Decision Log`, and `Outcomes & Retrospective` must be kept up to date as work proceeds.
100	
101	    If PLANS.md file is checked into the repo, reference the path to that file here from the repository root and note that this document must be maintained in accordance with PLANS.md.
102	
103	    ## Purpose / Big Picture
104	
105	    Explain in a few sentences what someone gains after this change and how they can see it working. State the user-visible behavior you will enable. Also explain what current complexity or interface burden this change removes.
106	
107	    ## Progress
108	
109	    Use a list with checkboxes to summarize granular steps. Every stopping point must be documented here, even if it requires splitting a partially completed task into two (“done” vs. “remaining”). This section must always reflect the actual current state of the work.
110	
111	    - [x] (2025-10-01 13:00Z) Example completed step.
112	    - [ ] Example incomplete step.
113	    - [ ] Example partially completed step (completed: X; remaining: Y).
114	
115	    Use timestamps to measure rates of progress.
116	
117	    ## Surprises & Discoveries
118	
119	    Document unexpected behaviors, bugs, optimizations, or insights discovered during implementation. Provide concise evidence.
120	
121	    - Observation: …
122	      Evidence: …
123	
124	    ## Decision Log
125	
126	    Record every decision made while working on the plan in the format:
127	
128	    - Decision: …
129	      Rationale: …
130	      Date/Author: …
131	
132	    ## Outcomes & Retrospective
133	
134	    Summarize outcomes, gaps, and lessons learned at major milestones or at completion. Compare the result against the original purpose.
135	
136	    ## Context and Orientation
137	
138	    Describe the current state relevant to this task as if the reader knows nothing. Name the key files and modules by full path. Define any non-obvious term you will use. Do not refer to prior plans. Make clear what callers or maintainers currently need to know that they should not need to know after the change.
139	
140	    ## Plan of Work
141	
142	    Describe, in prose, the sequence of edits and additions. For each edit, name the file and location (function, module) and what to insert or change. Keep it concrete and minimal. Explain how each major edit deepens a module, hides sequencing or policy, removes a special case, or otherwise reduces system complexity.
143	
144	    ## Concrete Steps
145	
146	    State the exact commands to run and where to run them (working directory). When a command generates output, show a short expected transcript so the reader can compare. This section must be updated as work proceeds.
147	
148	    ## Validation and Acceptance
149	
150	    Describe how to start or exercise the system and what to observe. Phrase acceptance as behavior, with specific inputs and outputs. If tests are involved, say "run <project’s test command> and expect <N> passed; the new test <name> fails before the change and passes after>".
151	
152	    ## Idempotence and Recovery
153	
154	    If steps can be repeated safely, say so. If a step is risky, provide a safe retry or rollback path. Keep the environment clean after completion.
155	
156	    ## Artifacts and Notes
157	
158	    Include the most important transcripts, diffs, or snippets as indented examples. Keep them concise and focused on what proves success.
159	
160	    ## Interfaces and Dependencies
161	
162	    Be prescriptive. Name the libraries, modules, and services to use and why. Specify the types, traits/interfaces, and function signatures that must exist at the end of the milestone. Prefer stable names and paths such as `crate::module::function` or `package.submodule.Interface`. For each major interface, say what detail it hides from its callers. E.g.:
163	
164	    In crates/foo/planner.rs, define:
165	
166	        pub trait Planner {
167	            fn plan(&self, observed: &Observed) -> Vec<Action>;
168	        }
169	
170	If you follow the guidance above, a single, stateless agent -- or a human novice -- can read your ExecPlan from top to bottom and produce a working, observable result. That is the bar: SELF-CONTAINED, SELF-SUFFICIENT, NOVICE-GUIDING, OUTCOME-FOCUSED.
171	
172	When you revise a plan, you must ensure your changes are comprehensively reflected across all sections, including the living document sections, and you must write a note at the bottom of the plan describing the change and the reason why. ExecPlans must describe not just the what but the why for almost everything.
173

1	# -*- coding: utf-8 -*- #

15	
16	"""The auth command gets tokens via oauth2."""
17	
18	
19	import textwrap
20	
21	from googlecloudsdk.api_lib.auth import external_account as auth_external_account
22	from googlecloudsdk.api_lib.auth import service_account as auth_service_account
23	from googlecloudsdk.api_lib.auth import util as auth_util
24	from googlecloudsdk.calliope import actions
25	from googlecloudsdk.calliope import base
26	from googlecloudsdk.calliope import exceptions as calliope_exceptions
27	from googlecloudsdk.command_lib.auth import auth_util as command_auth_util
28	from googlecloudsdk.command_lib.auth import flags as auth_flags
29	from googlecloudsdk.command_lib.auth import workforce_login_config as workforce_login_config_util
30	from googlecloudsdk.core import config
31	from googlecloudsdk.core import log
32	from googlecloudsdk.core import properties
33	from googlecloudsdk.core.console import console_io
34	from googlecloudsdk.core.credentials import creds as c_creds
35	from googlecloudsdk.core.credentials import devshell as c_devshell
36	from googlecloudsdk.core.credentials import exceptions as creds_exceptions
37	from googlecloudsdk.core.credentials import gce as c_gce
38	from googlecloudsdk.core.credentials import store as c_store
39	
40	
41	def ShouldContinueLogin(cred_config):
42	  """Prompts users if they try to login in managed environments.
43	
44	  Args:
45	    cred_config: Json object loaded from the file specified in --cred-file.
46	
47	  Returns:
48	    True if users want to continue the login command.
49	  """
50	  if c_devshell.IsDevshellEnvironment():
51	    message = textwrap.dedent("""
52	          You are already authenticated with gcloud when running
53	          inside the Cloud Shell and so do not need to run this
54	          command. Do you wish to proceed anyway?
55	          """)
56	    return console_io.PromptContinue(message=message)
57	  elif (c_gce.Metadata().connected and
58	        not auth_external_account.IsExternalAccountConfig(cred_config)):
59	    message = textwrap.dedent("""
60	          You are running on a Google Compute Engine virtual machine.
61	          It is recommended that you use service accounts for authentication.
62	
63	          You can run:
64	
65	            $ gcloud config set account `ACCOUNT`
66	
67	          to switch accounts if necessary.
68	
69	          Your credentials may be visible to others with access to this
70	          virtual machine. Are you sure you want to authenticate with
71	          your personal account?
72	          """)
73	    return console_io.PromptContinue(message=message)
74	  else:
75	    return True
76	
77	
78	def GetScopes(args):
79	  scopes = config.CLOUDSDK_SCOPES
80	  # Add REAUTH scope in case the user has 2fact activated.
81	  # This scope is only used here and when refreshing the access token.
82	  scopes += (config.REAUTH_SCOPE,)
83	
84	  if args.enable_gdrive_access:
85	    scopes += (auth_util.GOOGLE_DRIVE_SCOPE,)
86	  return scopes
87	
88	
89	def ShouldUseCachedCredentials(args, scopes):
90	  """If the login should use the locally cached account."""
91	  if (not args.account) or args.force:
92	    return False
93	  try:
94	    creds = c_store.Load(account=args.account, scopes=scopes)
95	  except creds_exceptions.Error:
96	    return False
97	  if not creds:
98	    return False
99	  # Account already has valid creds, just switch to it.
100	  log.warning('Re-using locally stored credentials for [{}]. '
101	              'To fetch new credentials, re-run the command with the '
102	              '`--force` flag.'.format(args.account))
103	  return True
104	
105	
106	@base.UniverseCompatible
107	class Login(base.Command):
108	  """Authorize gcloud to access the Cloud Platform with Google user credentials.
109	
110	  Obtains access credentials for your user account via a web-based authorization
111	  flow. When this command completes successfully, it sets the active account
112	  in the current configuration to the account specified. If no configuration
113	  exists, it creates a configuration named default.
114	
115	  If valid credentials for an account are already available from a prior
116	  authorization, the account is set to active without rerunning the flow.
117	
118	  Use `gcloud auth list` to view credentialed accounts.
119	
120	  If you'd rather authorize without a web browser but still interact with
121	  the command line, use the `--no-browser` flag. To authorize without
122	  a web browser and non-interactively, create a service account with the
123	  appropriate scopes using the
124	  [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com) and use
125	  `gcloud auth activate-service-account` with the corresponding JSON key file.
126	
127	  In addition to Google user credentials, authorization using workload identity
128	  federation, workforce identity federation, or service account keys is also
129	  supported.
130	
131	  For authorization with external accounts or service accounts, the
132	  `--cred-file` flag must be specified with the path
133	  to the workload identity credential configuration file or service account key
134	  file (JSON).
135	
136	  Login with workload and workforce identity federation is also supported in
137	  both gsutil and bq. This command is the recommended way of using external
138	  accounts.
139	
140	  For more information on workload identity federation, see:
141	  [](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workload-identity-federation).
142	
143	  For more information on workforce identity federation, see:
144	  [](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation).
145	
146	  For more information on authorization and credential types, see:
147	  [](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/authorizing).
148	
149	  ## EXAMPLES
150	
151	  To obtain access credentials for your user account, run:
152	
153	    $ {command}
154	
155	  To obtain access credentials using workload or workforce identity federation,
156	  run:
157	
158	    $ {command} --cred-file=/path/to/configuration/file
159	
160	  To obtain access credentials using a browser-based sign-in flow with workforce
161	  identity federation, run:
162	
163	    $ {command} --login-config=/path/to/configuration/file
164	  """
165	
166	  _remote_login_with_auth_proxy = False
167	
168	  @staticmethod
169	  def Args(parser):
170	    """Set args for gcloud auth."""
171	    parser.add_argument(
172	        '--activate',
173	        action='store_true',
174	        default=True,
175	        help='Set the new account to active.',
176	    )
177	    # --do-not-activate for (hidden) backwards compatibility.
178	    parser.add_argument(
179	        '--do-not-activate',
180	        action='store_false',
181	        dest='activate',
182	        hidden=True,
183	        help='THIS ARGUMENT NEEDS HELP TEXT.',
184	    )
185	    parser.add_argument(
186	        '--brief', action='store_true', help='Minimal user output.'
187	    )
188	    parser.add_argument(
189	        '--force',
190	        action='store_true',
191	        help=(
192	            'Re-run the web authorization flow even if the given account has '
193	            'valid credentials.'
194	        ),
195	    )
196	    parser.add_argument(
197	        '--enable-gdrive-access',
198	        action='store_true',
199	        help='Enable Google Drive access.',
200	    )
201	    parser.add_argument(
202	        '--update-adc',
203	        action='store_true',
204	        default=False,
205	        help=(
206	            'Write the obtained credentials to the well-known location for '
207	            'Application Default Credentials (ADC). Run '
208	            '$ gcloud auth application-default --help to learn more about ADC. '
209	            'Any credentials previously generated by '
210	            '`gcloud auth application-default login` will be overwritten.'
211	        ),
212	    )
213	    parser.add_argument(
214	        '--add-quota-project-to-adc',
215	        action='store_true',
216	        default=False,
217	        hidden=True,
218	        help=(
219	            "Read the project from gcloud's context and write to application "
220	            'default credentials as the quota project. Use this flag only '
221	            'when --update-adc is specified.'
222	        ),
223	    )
224	    parser.add_argument(
225	        'account',
226	        nargs='?',
227	        help=(
228	            'User account used for authorization. When the account specified '
229	            'has valid credentials in the local credential store these '
230	            'credentials will be re-used, otherwise a new credential will be '
231	            'fetched. If you need to fetch a new credential for an account '
232	            'with valid credentials stored, run the command with the --force '
233	            'flag.'
234	        )
235	    )
236	    parser.add_argument(
237	        '--cred-file',
238	        help=(
239	            'Path to the external account configuration file (workload identity'
240	            ' pool, generated by the Cloud Console or `gcloud iam'
241	            ' workload-identity-pools create-cred-config`) or service account'
242	            ' credential key file (JSON).'
243	        ),
244	    )
245	    parser.add_argument(
246	        '--login-config',
247	        help=(
248	            'Path to the workforce identity federation login configuration'
249	            ' file which can be generated using the `gcloud iam'
250	            ' workforce-pools create-login-config` command.'
251	        ),
252	        action=actions.StoreProperty(properties.VALUES.auth.login_config_file),
253	    )
254	
255	    auth_flags.AddRemoteLoginFlags(parser)
256	
257	    parser.display_info.AddFormat('none')
258	
259	  def Run(self, args):
260	    """Run the authentication command."""
261	    if args.cred_file:
262	      if args.login_config:
263	        raise calliope_exceptions.ConflictingArgumentsException(
264	            '--login-config cannot be specified with --cred-file.')
265	      cred_file = auth_util.GetCredentialsConfigFromFile(args.cred_file)
266	    else:
267	      cred_file = None
268	
269	    scopes = GetScopes(args)
270	
271	    if not ShouldContinueLogin(cred_file):
272	      return None
273	
274	    if args.cred_file:
275	      return LoginWithCredFileConfig(cred_file, scopes, args.project,
276	                                     args.activate, args.brief, args.update_adc,
277	                                     args.add_quota_project_to_adc,
278	                                     args.account)
279	    if ShouldUseCachedCredentials(args, scopes):
280	      creds = c_store.Load(account=args.account, scopes=scopes)
281	      return LoginAs(args.account, creds, args.project, args.activate,
282	                     args.brief, args.update_adc, args.add_quota_project_to_adc)
283	
284	    # No valid creds, do the web flow.
285	    flow_params = dict(
286	        no_launch_browser=not args.launch_browser,
287	        no_browser=not args.browser,
288	        remote_bootstrap=args.remote_bootstrap)
289	
290	    # 1. Try the 3PI web flow with --no-browser:
291	    #    This could be a 3PI flow initiated via --no-browser.
292	    #    If provider_name is present, then this is the 3PI flow.
293	    #    We can start the flow as is as the remote_bootstrap value will be used.
294	    if args.remote_bootstrap and 'provider_name' in args.remote_bootstrap:
295	      creds = auth_util.DoInstalledAppBrowserFlowGoogleAuth(
296	          config.CLOUDSDK_EXTERNAL_ACCOUNT_SCOPES,
297	          auth_proxy_redirect_uri='https://sdk.cloud.google/authcode.html',
298	          **flow_params
299	      )
300	      universe_domain_property = properties.VALUES.core.universe_domain
301	      if (
302	          creds
303	          and hasattr(creds, 'universe_domain')
304	          and creds.universe_domain != universe_domain_property.Get()
305	      ):
306	        # Get the account and handle universe domain conflict.
307	        account = auth_external_account.GetExternalAccountId(creds)
308	        auth_util.HandleUniverseDomainConflict(creds.universe_domain, account)
309	      return
310	    # 2. Try the 3PI web flow with a login configuration file.
311	    login_config_file = workforce_login_config_util.GetWorkforceLoginConfig()
312	    if login_config_file:
313	      if args.update_adc:
314	        raise calliope_exceptions.ConflictingArgumentsException(
315	            '--update-adc cannot be used in a third party login flow. '
316	            'Please use `gcloud auth application-default login`.')
317	      if args.add_quota_project_to_adc:
318	        raise calliope_exceptions.ConflictingArgumentsException(
319	            '--add-quota-project-to-adc cannot be used in a third party login '
320	            'flow.')
321	      creds = workforce_login_config_util.DoWorkforceHeadfulLogin(
322	          login_config_file,
323	          **flow_params)
324	
325	      account = auth_external_account.GetExternalAccountId(creds)
326	      c_store.Store(creds, account, config.CLOUDSDK_EXTERNAL_ACCOUNT_SCOPES)
327	      return LoginAs(account, creds, args.project, args.activate, args.brief,
328	                     args.update_adc, args.add_quota_project_to_adc)
329	
330	    # 3. Try the 1P web flow.
331	    creds = auth_util.DoInstalledAppBrowserFlowGoogleAuth(
332	        scopes,
333	        auth_proxy_redirect_uri='https://sdk.cloud.google.com/authcode.html',
334	        **flow_params)
335	    if not creds:
336	      return
337	    account = command_auth_util.ExtractAndValidateAccount(args.account, creds)
338	    # We got new creds, and they are for the correct user.
339	    c_store.Store(creds, account, scopes)
340	    return LoginAs(account, creds, args.project, args.activate, args.brief,
341	                   args.update_adc, args.add_quota_project_to_adc)
342	
343	
344	def LoginWithCredFileConfig(cred_config, scopes, project, activate, brief,
345	                            update_adc, add_quota_project_to_adc, args_account):
346	  """Login with the provided configuration loaded from --cred-file.
347	
348	  Args:
349	    cred_config (Mapping): The configuration dictionary representing the
350	      credentials. This is loaded from the --cred-file argument.
351	    scopes (Tuple[str]): The default OAuth scopes to use.
352	    project (Optional[str]): The optional project ID to activate / persist.
353	    activate (bool): Whether to set the new account associated with the
354	      credentials to active.
355	    brief (bool): Whether to use minimal user output.
356	    update_adc (bool): Whether to write the obtained credentials to the
357	      well-known location for Application Default Credentials (ADC).
358	    add_quota_project_to_adc (bool): Whether to add the quota project to the
359	      application default credentials file.
360	    args_account (Optional[str]): The optional ACCOUNT argument. When provided,
361	      this should match the account ID on the authenticated credentials.
362	
363	  Returns:
364	    google.auth.credentials.Credentials: The authenticated stored credentials.
365	
366	  Raises:
367	    calliope_exceptions.ConflictingArgumentsException: If conflicting arguments
368	      are provided.
369	    calliope_exceptions.InvalidArgumentException: If invalid arguments are
370	      provided.
371	  """
372	  # Remove reauth scope (only applicable to 1P user accounts).
373	  scopes = tuple(x for x in scopes if x != config.REAUTH_SCOPE)
374	  # Reject unsupported arguments.
375	  if add_quota_project_to_adc:
376	    raise calliope_exceptions.ConflictingArgumentsException(
377	        '[--add-quota-project-to-adc] cannot be specified with --cred-file')
378	  if auth_external_account.IsExternalAccountConfig(cred_config):
379	    creds = auth_external_account.CredentialsFromAdcDictGoogleAuth(cred_config)
380	    account = auth_external_account.GetExternalAccountId(creds)
381	    # Check interactive mode, if true, inject the _tokeninfo_username.
382	    # TODO(b/258323440): Once the SDK can handle the retrieving token info, we
383	    # don't need this injection any more.
384	    if hasattr(creds, 'interactive') and creds.interactive:
385	      setattr(creds, '_tokeninfo_username', account)
386	  elif auth_service_account.IsServiceAccountConfig(cred_config):
387	    creds = auth_service_account.CredentialsFromAdcDictGoogleAuth(cred_config)
388	    account = creds.service_account_email
389	  else:
390	    raise calliope_exceptions.InvalidArgumentException(
391	        '--cred-file',
392	        'Only external account or service account JSON credential file types '
393	        'are supported.')
394	
395	  if args_account and args_account != account:
396	    raise calliope_exceptions.InvalidArgumentException(
397	        'ACCOUNT',
398	        'The given account name does not match the account name in the '
399	        'credential file. This argument can be omitted when using '
400	        'credential files.')
401	  # Check if account already exists in storage.
402	  try:
403	    # prevent_refresh must be set to True. We don't actually want to
404	    # use the existing credentials, but check for their presence. If a
405	    # refresh occurs but the credentials are no longer valid, this
406	    # will cause gcloud to crash.
407	    exist_creds = c_store.Load(
408	        account=account, scopes=scopes, prevent_refresh=True)
409	  except creds_exceptions.Error:
410	    exist_creds = None
411	  if exist_creds and exist_creds.universe_domain == creds.universe_domain:
412	    message = textwrap.dedent("""
413	      You are already authenticated with '%s'.
414	      Do you wish to proceed and overwrite existing credentials?
415	      """)
416	    answer = console_io.PromptContinue(message=message % account, default=True)
417	    if not answer:
418	      return None
419	  # Store credentials and activate if --activate is true.
420	  c_store.Store(creds, account, scopes=scopes)
421	  return LoginAs(account, creds, project, activate, brief, update_adc, False)
422	
423	
424	def LoginAs(account, creds, project, activate, brief, update_adc,
425	            add_quota_project_to_adc):
426	  """Logs in with valid credentials."""
427	  if hasattr(creds, 'universe_domain'):
428	    auth_util.HandleUniverseDomainConflict(creds.universe_domain, account)
429	
430	  _ValidateADCFlags(update_adc, add_quota_project_to_adc)
431	  if update_adc:
432	    _UpdateADC(creds, add_quota_project_to_adc)
433	  if not activate:
434	    return creds
435	  properties.PersistProperty(properties.VALUES.core.account, account)
436	  if project:
437	    properties.PersistProperty(properties.VALUES.core.project, project)
438	
439	  if not brief:
440	    if c_creds.IsExternalAccountCredentials(creds):
441	      confirmation_msg = (
442	          'Authenticated with external account credentials for: [{0}].'.format(
443	              account))
444	    elif c_creds.IsExternalAccountUserCredentials(creds):
445	      confirmation_msg = (
446	          'Authenticated with external account user credentials for: '
447	          '[{0}].'.format(account))
448	    elif c_creds.IsServiceAccountCredentials(creds):
449	      confirmation_msg = (
450	          'Authenticated with service account credentials for: [{0}].'.format(
451	              account))
452	    elif c_creds.IsExternalAccountAuthorizedUserCredentials(creds):
453	      confirmation_msg = (
454	          'Authenticated with external account authorized user credentials '
455	          'for: [{0}].'.format(account))
456	    else:
457	      confirmation_msg = 'You are now logged in as [{0}].'.format(account)
458	    log.status.write(
459	        '\n{confirmation_msg}\n'
460	        'Your current project is [{project}].  You can change this setting '
461	        'by running:\n  $ gcloud config set project PROJECT_ID\n'.format(
462	            confirmation_msg=confirmation_msg,
463	            project=properties.VALUES.core.project.Get()))
464	  return creds
465	
466	
467	def _UpdateADC(creds, add_quota_project_to_adc):
468	  """Updates the ADC json with the credentials creds."""
469	  old_adc_json = command_auth_util.GetADCAsJson()
470	  command_auth_util.WriteGcloudCredentialsToADC(creds, add_quota_project_to_adc)
471	  new_adc_json = command_auth_util.GetADCAsJson()
472	  if new_adc_json and new_adc_json != old_adc_json:
473	    adc_msg = '\nApplication Default Credentials (ADC) were updated.'
474	    quota_project = command_auth_util.GetQuotaProjectFromADC()
475	    if quota_project:
476	      adc_msg = adc_msg + (
477	          "\n'{}' is added to ADC as the quota project.\nTo "
478	          'just update the quota project in ADC, use $gcloud auth '
479	          'application-default set-quota-project.'.format(quota_project))
480	    log.status.Print(adc_msg)
481	
482	
483	def _ValidateADCFlags(update_adc, add_quota_project_to_adc):
484	  if not update_adc and add_quota_project_to_adc:
485	    raise calliope_exceptions.InvalidArgumentException(
486	        '--add-quota-project-to-adc',
487	        '--add-quota-project-to-adc cannot be specified without specifying '
488	        '--update-adc.'
489	    )
490

15	"""Update or add a quota project in application default credentials json."""
16	
17	
18	from googlecloudsdk.calliope import base
19	from googlecloudsdk.command_lib.auth import auth_util
20	from googlecloudsdk.command_lib.resource_manager import completers
21	
22	
23	class SetQuotaProject(base.SilentCommand):
24	  """Update or add a quota project in application default credentials (ADC).
25	
26	  A quota project is a Google Cloud Project that will be used for billing
27	  and quota limits.
28	
29	  Before running this command, an ADC must already be generated using
30	  $ gcloud auth application-default login.
31	  The quota project defined in the ADC will be used by the Google client
32	  libraries.
33	  The existing application default credentials must have the
34	  `serviceusage.services.use` permission on the given project.
35	
36	  ## EXAMPLES
37	
38	  To update the quota project in application default credentials to
39	  `my-quota-project`, run:
40	
41	    $ {command} my-quota-project
42	  """
43	
44	  @staticmethod
45	  def Args(parser):
46	    base.Argument(
47	        'quota_project_id',
48	        metavar='QUOTA_PROJECT_ID',
49	        completer=completers.ProjectCompleter,
50	        help='Quota project ID to add to application default credentials. If '
51	        'a quota project already exists, it will be updated.').AddToParser(
52	            parser)
53	
54	  def Run(self, args):
55	    return auth_util.AddQuotaProjectToADC(args.quota_project_id)
56

content.text: Critical findings emerging. Note `login.py` uses `--update-adc` (not `--no-activate`; the flag is `--activate` with `--do-not-activate` hidden). The plan uses `--no-activate` which does NOT exist. Let me verify the flag names, and read `auth_util.py` (the command_lib one) for ADC write behavior and quota rollback.
